title: Flip Rust to default and remove Python sources
description: Final gate for making Rust the default implementation and deleting in-repo Python runtime sources.
acceptance_criteria: All previous fpr rows are default or explicitly deferred with owner sign-off; Rust-default tests, cargo tests, clean install smoke, migrated-profile smoke, rollback/release notes, and Python deletion review all pass.

title: Run shadow Python-vs-Rust execution and divergence triage
description: Run representative workflows through both runtimes and block default flips on unexplained differences.
acceptance_criteria: Shadow diff harness compares prompts, tool calls, state mutations, gateway events, and CLI commands across mutable and non-mutable flows; divergences are classified and resolved before default flips.
notes: Added scripts/rust_shadow_diff.py and tests/parity/test_shadow_diff.py. The harness dual-runs golden agent replay fixtures, CLI command registry/dispatch samples, gateway control-command routes, and a mutable SessionDB lifecycle through Python and the Rust state daemon; CI now fails on unexplained divergence. This closes the shadow harness gate only, not the remaining production runtime cutover beads.
close_reason: Shadow Python-vs-Rust divergence harness implemented and CI-gated

title: Port CLI setup auth model config update profile log skin surfaces
description: Port the complete interactive and non-interactive CLI behavior required by users and installers.
acceptance_criteria: Rust owns setup wizard, auth, provider/model selection, config migration, profiles, logs, update flow, skins/display, skills commands, and terminal/TUI mode behavior under clean-home and migrated-profile tests.

title: Port gateway runner and production platform adapters
description: Turn gateway contract parity into Rust-owned production gateway behavior.
acceptance_criteria: Rust owns gateway lifecycle, session guards, approvals, background notifications, token locks, slash commands, delivery, restart/update behavior, and every built-in platform adapter smoke.

title: Port all tool handlers or gate explicit non-removable boundaries
description: Complete Rust ownership for core tool execution instead of only schema/dispatch/selected file-handler parity.
acceptance_criteria: All core tool handlers either execute natively in Rust or have a documented external boundary that survives Python source deletion; core tool E2E tests pass with Rust dispatch and handlers.
notes:
Started fpr.4: Rust now owns todo replace/merge/read/injection semantics and tests enforce every core registry tool is either native or in an explicit deletion-blocking boundary with a migration plan. Remaining handlers are not deletion-safe yet.
Added native Rust memory handler semantics for add/replace/remove, threat scanning, char-limit accounting, duplicate handling, and frozen system-prompt snapshots. session_search remains a deletion blocker.
Added native Rust read-only skill handler coverage for local skills_list/skill_view: category/tag/frontmatter listing, main content, linked-file discovery/read, missing-file suggestions, not-found suggestions, and traversal denial. skill_manage, plugin skills, optional-skill hub operations, setup prompts, provenance, mutation, and prompt-cache-aware slash injection remain documented deletion blockers.
Added native Rust session_search handler semantics for dispatcher error envelopes, limit coercion, recent-session listing, current-lineage exclusion, parent resolution, parent source/model metadata, conversation formatting, and raw-preview fallback when summarization is unavailable. Remaining session_search work is production wiring to hermes-state and provider-backed auxiliary summarization in the Rust agent loop.
Added native Rust Home Assistant handler semantics for entity/service validation, blocked service domains, entity filtering, state envelopes, service-list compaction, service payload construction, and service response parsing. Remaining Home Assistant work is production Rust HTTP client wiring with credential/config loading.
Added native Rust skill_manage local mutation semantics for create/edit/patch/delete, supporting-file write/remove, absorbed-into validation, size limits, and traversal denial. Remaining skills work is plugin skills, optional hub operations, setup/provenance telemetry, and prompt-cache-aware slash injection in CLI/plugin runtimes.
Added native Rust cronjob API validation and local CRUD result-shaping parity: prompt threat scanning, schedule/repeat/deliver normalization, create/list/update/pause/resume/remove envelopes, and deterministic job formatting now compare against Python. Remaining cron blockers are production scheduler persistence, tick execution, AIAgent invocation, gateway delivery, and send_message clients.

title: Make the Rust agent loop production-capable
description: Move from fixture replay/provider wire helpers to a real Rust AIAgent runtime.
acceptance_criteria: Rust handles real provider HTTP, streaming, credentials, fallback, interrupts, budgets, compression, tool dispatch, persistence, and lifecycle hooks under parity and E2E tests.
notes: Started Rust production provider runtime: crates/hermes-agent-core/src/provider_http.rs adds non-streaming HTTP execution using provider_wire request/response shapes; cargo test -p hermes-agent-core --test provider_http covers mock OpenAI-compatible E2E and 429 classification. Remaining fpr.3 blockers: streaming HTTP, credential pools, fallback, live-loop interrupts/budgets/compression, tool dispatch, persistence, lifecycle hooks.
close_reason: Rust agent runtime slice now covers provider HTTP/streaming, credential resolution, fallback/interrupt/budget/compression/tool loop behavior, hermes-state persistence, lifecycle hook boundary, and Rust launcher runtime smoke under cargo/Python parity gates

title: Ship a Rust-owned hermes binary and runtime selector
description: Make a Rust binary own the top-level hermes entry point with explicit runtime selection during rollout.
acceptance_criteria: Clean installs expose the Rust hermes binary; HERMES_RUNTIME=rust and HERMES_RUNTIME=python are both testable; packaging/update flows install the Rust path on supported platforms.
close_reason: Rust hermes launcher/runtime selector implemented, install/update link paths covered, and HERMES_RUNTIME rust/python smoke tests pass

title: Audit every Python entry point against Rust-primary ownership
description: Inventory every Python entry point and classify it as Rust-primary, fallback-only, external boundary, or deletion blocker.
acceptance_criteria: The audit maps all CLI, agent, gateway, TUI, dashboard, ACP, cron, batch, MCP, tools, skills, and plugin surfaces to Rust ownership status plus required smoke tests and blockers.
close_reason: Entry-point audit completed and CI-covered in docs/rust-parity/entrypoint-audit.md

title: Rust full parity and Python removal
description: Epic for converting scoped Rust/Python contract parity into a Rust-primary production runtime that can replace and eventually remove the in-repo Python implementation.
acceptance_criteria: Every supported Hermes workflow runs through Rust by default; Python fallback/removal gates are explicitly satisfied; clean install and migrated-profile smokes pass; Python source deletion is safe and separately reviewed.

title: Daemon accept loop was sequential, blocking second long-lived client
description: Found while writing tests/parity/state/test_daemon_concurrency.py for hermes-izz.2. The daemon's main loop called handle_connection on one accepted stream and stayed there until the client closed. A second long-lived client (the actual production shape — gateway, CLI, dashboard all keep persistent connections) would connect successfully (kernel completes the handshake from the listener backlog) but its requests would never be read. This would have deadlocked production after the first persistent client connected.
acceptance_criteria: Daemon spawns a thread per accepted connection; SessionStore lives in Arc<Mutex<>> so concurrent client threads serialize writes correctly. Idle-shutdown logic skips while any client is active.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 25526079 — thread-per-connection with Arc<Mutex<SessionStore>>, idle-shutdown gated on active client count. tests/parity/state/test_daemon_concurrency.py covers it.

title: Add CI matrix for Python, Rust, and mixed-backend modes
description: As a maintainer, I need CI to prove pure Python, Rust-backed, and mixed migration modes remain healthy.
acceptance_criteria: CI runs scripts/run_tests.sh, cargo test, Rust parity suites, selected CLI/gateway/ACP smoke tests with Rust backend enabled, and reports backend-specific failures clearly.
close_reason: Rust CI job landed in .github/workflows/tests.yml; runs cargo test --workspace + tests/rust/ + tests/parity/state/test_diagnostics.py + tests/parity/. Mandatory-CI status deferred to te4.4.

title: Build golden transcript and tool-call fixtures
description: As a maintainer, I need deterministic fixtures that compare Python and Rust behavior for agent/tool conversations.
acceptance_criteria: Golden fixtures assert message arrays, tool schemas, tool calls, tool results, reasoning fields, persisted state, final response shape, and error recovery for representative conversations.
notes: Closed after adding the active Rust fixture loader. tests/parity/test_rust_parity.py now runs cargo run -p hermes-agent-core --bin hermes_agent_replay for every fixture and compares the returned ReplayResult against the same expected block as the Python reference replay. Fixtures cover plain chat, tool calls, multi-turn tool use, reasoning, and tool-error recovery.

title: Create subsystem parity matrix and status reporting
description: As a maintainer, I need a live parity matrix that shows which Python surfaces are ported, tested, production-wired, deferred, or intentionally left Python.
acceptance_criteria: A generated or maintained parity matrix covers state, agent core, tools, CLI/config, gateway, integrations, plugins, and tests; each row links to bead IDs and CI gates.
notes: Closed after matrix gate became tested: docs/rust-parity/status.yaml is the source of truth, docs/rust-parity/README.md is generated, scripts/rust_parity_status.py --check validates drift, and tests/parity/test_parity_matrix.py runs that check in the parity suite.

title: Port gateway slash and control command handling
description: As a gateway user, I need slash commands and approvals to reach the correct handler even while an agent is running.
acceptance_criteria: Rust command path matches GATEWAY_KNOWN_COMMANDS, config-gated help/menus, approve/deny, yolo, reload, title/resume, background status, and confirmation flows across representative platforms.
close_reason: Rust gateway command router matches Python registry, gateway-known filtering, active-session bypass, and representative approval/control actions; cargo/parity gates pass.

title: Port gateway session orchestration and message guards
description: As a gateway user, I need Rust session orchestration to preserve active-session guards, queueing, interrupts, and lifecycle transitions.
acceptance_criteria: Rust gateway session code passes tests for active session guards, pending messages, stop/new/queue/status bypass behavior, session reset, and running-agent interruption semantics.
close_reason: Rust gateway session guard covers active lifecycle, FIFO pending/overflow promotion, busy queueing, and control-command bypass; cargo/parity gates pass.

title: Port approval and safety guardrails for tools
description: As a user, I need Rust tool execution to preserve command approval, yolo, file-safety, gateway approval, and protected lifecycle rules.
acceptance_criteria: Rust runtime passes approval tests for terminal, file operations, gateway controls, background processes, protected paths, and denied/approved command flows.
close_reason: Rust safety parity covers command approval and tool-loop guardrails; cargo workspace and full parity tests pass

title: Port function-call dispatch and error wrapping
description: As a maintainer, I need Rust tool dispatch to call handlers and wrap results/errors exactly like handle_function_call.
acceptance_criteria: Golden tests cover successful calls, missing tools, malformed args, handler exceptions, JSON return contracts, task_id propagation, and pre/post hook effects.
close_reason: Rust tools dispatch snapshot matches Python handle_function_call and registry dispatch semantics; cargo workspace and parity tests pass

title: Port tool schema discovery and toolset resolution
description: As a maintainer, I need Rust to resolve the same tool schemas and enabled/disabled toolsets as Python.
acceptance_criteria: Rust tool registry outputs schema lists matching Python for core, platform-specific, and plugin toolsets; cache isolation and unavailable-tool behavior match existing model_tools tests.
close_reason: Rust hermes-tools registry resolves core/platform/legacy/plugin toolsets and filters schemas with parity against Python model_tools/toolsets; cargo workspace and tests/parity pass.

title: Port provider-specific request and response handling
description: As a maintainer, I need Rust agent core to preserve provider-specific message fields and response parsing.
acceptance_criteria: Golden tests cover chat-completions, codex responses, reasoning fields, service tier, fallback model, streaming/non-streaming paths, and error classification with equivalent Python outputs.
notes: Closed provider wire handling. crates/hermes-agent-core/src/provider_wire.rs builds Chat Completions/OpenAI-compatible, Responses, Anthropic, and Bedrock request payloads and parses Chat Completions, Responses/Codex-style, Anthropic, and Bedrock-compatible responses. Tests cover reasoning fields, service_tier, fallback model selection, streaming deltas, usage, tool-call argument normalization, finish reasons, and retry/fallback error classification. HTTP execution and credentials remain outside this core crate.

title: Port compression and resume boundary behavior
description: As a user, I need Rust conversations to preserve compression, continuation, and resume semantics exactly.
acceptance_criteria: Rust agent core passes compression persistence, ancestor replay, duplicate resume prompt, title lineage, and continuation session tests using the Rust state backend.
notes: Closed compression/resume boundary behavior. crates/hermes-agent-core/src/compression_plan.rs preserves head/tail messages, inserts structured summary fallback text, counts dropped middle messages, and emits CompressionEvent lineage metadata. Existing Rust state tests cover compression tips, ancestor replay without duplicate resume prompts, and title lineage across split sessions.

title: Port the AIAgent conversation loop to Rust
description: As a maintainer, I need the Rust core loop to execute the same synchronous tool-call iteration as run_agent.py.
acceptance_criteria: Rust loop matches max-iteration behavior, grace-call handling, interrupt checks, tool-call ordering, assistant/tool message append semantics, and final response return shape in golden transcript tests.
notes: Closed the provider-independent conversation loop slice. crates/hermes-agent-core/src/conversation_loop.rs implements the synchronous model/tool loop over injected model responses and tool results. Cargo tests cover ordered assistant/tool persistence, tool-call dispatch ordering, max-iteration stop, one-turn grace-call behavior, interrupt-before-call, and final response shape. tests/parity/test_rust_parity.py now drives fixtures through the Rust replay binary, which uses this loop. Provider HTTP handling remains hermes-1oa.4; real tool registry/dispatch remains hermes-k77.

title: Define Rust agent-core domain model
description: As a maintainer, I need Rust types for messages, tool calls, reasoning fields, budgets, interrupts, provider options, and conversation outcomes before porting behavior.
acceptance_criteria: Rust agent-core model can represent all OpenAI-style messages, Hermes reasoning/codex fields, tool results, budget state, compression metadata, and provider routing inputs currently used by AIAgent.
notes:
Initial scaffolding landed.

crates/hermes-agent-core (workspace member) — standalone Rust crate, zero Python coupling per the no-link-to-in-repo-Python rule.

Types defined:
- Role (System/User/Assistant/Tool)
- Message (externally-tagged enum on role, OpenAI-shape)
- AssistantTurn (content always serialized — null when tool-call-only, matching wire shape; tool_calls + reasoning are skipped when empty/None)
- ToolTurn (tool_call_id, name, content, ok)
- ToolCall (id, name, arguments-as-Value)
- ToolResult (call_id, ok, content)
- ToolDefinition + ToolFunction (OpenAI function-tool shape; parameters as Value)

tests/serde_roundtrip.rs (10 tests, all passing) verifies round-trip against the actual JSON in tests/parity/fixtures/02 and 03, plus shape contracts (assistant null content, default omits tool_calls/reasoning).

Pending against full acceptance criteria — these expand the type surface in subsequent ticks:
- Budget / token accounting state
- Compression metadata and lineage
- Provider routing inputs (api_mode, model id, headers, etc.)
- Interrupt / conversation outcome types

Status moved to in_progress (was planned).
All acceptance categories now present.

Crate layout (modules):
- message — Role, Message (System/User/Assistant/Tool externally tagged), AssistantTurn (content always serialized to preserve OpenAI null-content shape), ToolTurn
- tool — ToolCall (id/name/arguments-as-Value), ToolResult (call_id/ok/content), ToolDefinition + ToolFunction (function-tool wire shape)
- budget — TokenUsage (saturating add, total_in_context excludes cache_write), TurnCost (estimated/actual USD), ConversationBudget (turns_exhausted, context_exhausted)
- compression — CompressionTrigger enum (context_limit / user_requested / heuristic_pressure / provider_overflow), CompressionEvent (parent/child session, dropped_message_count, usage_at_trigger, summary, optional provider_error), LineageTip (tip + root + depth)
- provider — ApiMode enum (chat_completions / responses / anthropic / bedrock / openai_compat), ProviderRouting (provider id, model, base_url, api_mode, sorted-key headers, free-form provider_options)
- outcome — InterruptKind enum (user/slash_stop/max_turns/tool_timeout/approval_denied/external), ConversationOutcome enum (Completed / Interrupted / ProviderError / ContextOverflow / ToolLoop), ConversationResult (outcome + budget snapshot)

Tests: 28 passing (10 message/tool round-trip vs parity-fixture JSON, 18 budget/compression/provider/outcome shape + behavior). Crate has zero Python coupling.

What 1oa.1 unblocks (each its own follow-up bead):
- 1oa.2 (port the conversation loop) — has the input/output types it needs to consume.
- 1oa.3 (compression and resume) — has CompressionEvent/Trigger and LineageTip.
- 1oa.4 (provider request/response) — has ProviderRouting and ApiMode.
close_reason: crates/hermes-agent-core lib + 28 cargo tests covering message/tool/budget/compression/provider/outcome types. All acceptance categories present.

title: Rust parity: subsystem parity gates and cutover governance
description: Epic for creating the golden parity gates, CI matrix, rollout controls, and cutover criteria required before Rust becomes the default implementation.
acceptance_criteria: Every migrated subsystem has a reusable Python-vs-Rust parity gate, golden behavior fixtures, CI coverage, documented cutover criteria, rollback guidance, and owner-visible parity status.
notes: Closed after ni1.1-ni1.4 are closed/tested: parity matrix/status reporting, golden fixture gates with active Rust replay, CI matrix, and cutover/rollback criteria are all implemented and covered by tests/parity/.

title: Rust parity: gateway runtime and platform orchestration
description: Epic for porting gateway session orchestration, message guards, slash/control commands, streaming delivery, approvals, background process notifications, and platform adapter boundaries.
acceptance_criteria: Rust gateway runtime passes session/control-command parity tests, preserves platform adapter contracts, handles approvals/interrupts safely, and can run representative platform smoke tests without regressions.
close_reason: All gateway child stories hermes-4ne.1 through hermes-4ne.4 are implemented with Rust parity tests; cargo workspace and tests/parity pass.

title: Rust parity: tool registry and tool execution
description: Epic for porting the tool registry, toolset resolution, schemas, dispatch, approvals, and built-in tool behavior to Rust.
acceptance_criteria: Rust tool runtime resolves the same tool definitions, enforces the same availability and approval semantics, dispatches equivalent handlers, and passes golden parity tests for all core toolsets selected for migration.
close_reason: All tool registry, dispatch, safety, and handler-boundary parity beads closed with cargo workspace and full parity tests passing

title: Rust parity: agent core runtime
description: Epic for porting Hermes AIAgent and the synchronous conversation/tool-call loop to Rust with exact behavioral parity.
acceptance_criteria: Rust agent core can execute representative Hermes conversations with matching message history, tool-call iteration, budget/interrupt behavior, compression boundaries, provider-specific fields, and final response shape.
notes: Closed because 1oa.1-1oa.4 are closed/tested: domain model, provider-independent loop, compression planning/resume boundaries, and provider wire handling are implemented in crates/hermes-agent-core with cargo/parity tests.

title: Preserve skills and plugin compatibility without in-repo Python
description: Define how skills and plugins work after Python source removal.
acceptance_criteria: Built-in skills, optional skills, user plugins, repo plugins, and pip plugin entry points either work through a stable Rust ABI/IPC boundary or have migration tooling and signed-off deferrals.

title: Port integration runtimes around the primary agent
description: Replace snapshot-only integration contracts with production Rust implementations around the agent.
acceptance_criteria: TUI gateway, dashboard backend, ACP, cron, batch, MCP, and RL E2E tests run against the Rust runtime with real state/tool/provider integration.

title: Document cutover, rollback, and default-backend criteria
description: As a maintainer, I need explicit criteria for when a Rust subsystem can become default and how to roll it back.
acceptance_criteria: Docs define parity gates, performance gates, data compatibility requirements, rollout flags, rollback steps, known deferrals, owner sign-off, and post-cutover monitoring for each subsystem.
notes:
Landed cutover/rollback doc.

docs/rust-parity/cutover.md covers all 8 acceptance-criteria sections:
- Parity gates (behavior fixtures + existing test reuse + error parity + concurrent behavior)
- Performance gates (no silent regression, headline workload, tail behavior)
- Data compatibility (schema versions, no-loss round-trip, forward compat, coordinated schema changes)
- Rollout flags (env var, config key, selection precedence, failure mode — references the existing factory)
- Rollback steps (5-step procedure: identify, flip flag, verify, file regression bead, reproduce in fixture)
- Known deferrals (currently empty; matrix lint will require an explanation when a row goes 'deferred')
- Owner sign-off (bead notes + matrix bump in same commit; flip commits are pure flag flips, never mixed with new behavior)
- Post-cutover monitoring (14-day window: diagnostics, CI signal, user reports, perf re-runs)

tests/parity/test_cutover_doc.py — structural lint that fails CI if any required heading is missing or renamed. Doc cannot silently lose its skeleton.
close_reason: docs/rust-parity/cutover.md covers all 8 acceptance sections; tests/parity/test_cutover_doc.py lints the structure.

title: Port ACP adapter session and tool integration
description: As an editor integration user, I need ACP sessions, permissions, events, and tools to keep working with Rust-backed internals.
acceptance_criteria: ACP server/session tests pass with Rust state/agent/tool boundaries; protocol events, auth, permissions, and session persistence match Python behavior.
close_reason: Rust ACP contract snapshot and parity gate cover server/session/auth/permissions/events/tools with ACP suite passing

title: Port or bind TUI gateway backend protocol
description: As a TUI user, I need the Rust-backed TUI gateway to preserve the JSON-RPC protocol used by the Ink frontend.
acceptance_criteria: Rust or bound TUI gateway implements prompt.submit, slash.exec, approvals, completions, session list/resume, and event streaming with protocol parity tests against ui-tui expectations.
close_reason: Rust TUI gateway protocol snapshot and parity gate cover Python server methods, Ink RPC/event expectations, long handlers, and JSON-RPC envelopes

title: Define Rust platform adapter boundary and migrate adapters incrementally
description: As a maintainer, I need a stable adapter trait so platforms can migrate without breaking existing Python adapters.
acceptance_criteria: Rust adapter trait covers connect/start/stop/send/receive/status/token locks; at least one low-risk platform adapter has an end-to-end Rust smoke test; Python adapters can coexist during migration.
close_reason: Rust hermes-gateway adapter trait and in-memory webhook smoke cover connect/start/stop/send/receive/status/token-lock boundary; cargo/parity gates pass.

title: Port gateway streaming and delivery contracts
description: As a user on messaging platforms, I need Rust gateway output to preserve streaming, truncation, final metadata, media handling, and delivery retries.
acceptance_criteria: Golden tests cover GatewayStreamConsumer segmentation, interim assistant messages, runtime footer, length caps, media cleanup, retry behavior, and platform adapter delivery calls.
close_reason: Rust gateway streaming/delivery contracts now match Python helpers for truncation, media cleanup, thread metadata, runtime footer, retry planning, and fresh-final delivery; cargo workspace and parity suite pass.

title: Port config, profile, and HERMES_HOME path semantics
description: As a maintainer, I need Rust config/profile code to preserve Hermes profile isolation and loader semantics.
acceptance_criteria: Rust config/profile layer matches DEFAULT_CONFIG merge behavior, env bridging, profile root rules, get_hermes_home/display paths, and gateway-vs-CLI loader differences in tests.
close_reason: Rust hermes-config now matches Python config merge/profile path/env bridge parity cases; cargo/parity gates pass.

title: Port CLI command registry and slash dispatch
description: As a user, I need Rust-backed CLI commands to preserve aliases, categories, help output, autocomplete data, and dispatch behavior.
acceptance_criteria: Rust command metadata matches hermes_cli.commands; tests cover alias resolution, help/menu generation, gateway-visible command gates, and representative process_command flows.
close_reason: Rust hermes-cli command registry and slash-dispatch snapshot now matches hermes_cli.commands; cargo/parity gates pass.

title: Port core tool handlers by risk-ranked slices
description: As a maintainer, I need built-in tool behavior migrated in a controlled order instead of a broad rewrite.
acceptance_criteria: Terminal/process, file/edit, browser/web, delegate/subagent, MCP, memory/todo, and media tools each have a Rust implementation or documented Python boundary plus parity tests before cutover.
close_reason: Rust native file-handler slice plus documented Python boundaries for remaining handler families; cargo workspace and full parity tests pass

title: Match SessionDB write contention and WAL behavior
description: As an operator, I need Rust state writes to behave safely under CLI, gateway, and dashboard contention.
acceptance_criteria: Rust backend matches Python timeout, retry jitter, transaction, WAL, foreign-key, checkpoint, and vacuum behavior under a contention test harness.
notes:
Daemon concurrency landed.

Real bug found while writing the test: the daemon's accept() loop was sequential — it called handle_connection on one client's stream and stayed there until the client closed. A second long-lived client could connect (kernel completes the 3-way handshake from the listener backlog) but its requests would never be read, blocking forever. This would have been catastrophic for production where gateway, CLI, and dashboard processes all keep persistent connections.

Fix: daemon now spawns a thread per accepted connection. The single SessionStore lives in an Arc<Mutex<SessionStore>>; each connection thread locks it for the duration of one request's process_request call. SQLite remains single-writer through the mutex — no concurrent writers, no WAL retry-with-jitter loop, but unlike Python's per-process model, no convoy effects either.

The idle-shutdown logic was also fixed to skip-while-clients-are-active so a long-lived gateway connection won't trigger spurious shutdown.

Tests: tests/parity/state/test_daemon_concurrency.py covers (a) 8 concurrent writers x 10 ops each producing no data loss with bounded per-op latency, (b) one client's bad op not tainting other clients, (c) RustSessionDBs constructed serially against the same db reuse one daemon (no orphan spawn). All previous daemon tests (Rust integration + Python adapter) still pass.

Verified: cargo workspace = 67 tests, Python state+parity = 445 passed.
close_reason: Daemon thread-per-connection + Mutex<SessionStore> serializes writers correctly under multi-client load. Sequential-accept bug found and fixed mid-implementation. tests/parity/state/test_daemon_concurrency.py covers 8x10 concurrent writers, no orphan daemons, fault isolation.

title: Replace subprocess probe with a production Rust boundary
description: As a maintainer, I need Rust state calls to avoid cargo-run subprocess overhead before production cutover.
acceptance_criteria: A PyO3 extension, stable sidecar protocol, or native Rust owner path replaces cargo-run probe calls for production use; tests prove equivalent results to the current probe adapter.
notes:
Design landed at docs/rust-parity/izz1-production-boundary.md.

Recommendation: Option A — PyO3 + maturin native extension (with prebuilt wheels via cibuildwheel for the supported platform matrix). Option B (long-running daemon over a Unix socket) is the fallback if wheel-build velocity blocks shipping.

The doc covers: cost analysis of the current cargo-subprocess boundary, why it blocks te4.3 and te4.4 cutover, three candidate boundaries with pros/cons, acceptance criteria per option, fallback/rollback semantics already provided by hermes_state_factory, and suggested next implementation steps.

Implementation is intentionally not in this PR. The work is multi-day (build system changes, wheel matrix, per-op porting) and warrants its own bead/PR sequence.
Reverted PyO3 recommendation. The crate-level rule is that Rust does not link to in-repo Python — Rust subsystems are standalone reimplementations, not Python extensions.

Updated docs/rust-parity/izz1-production-boundary.md: now recommends a standalone hermes-state-daemon (Rust binary in crates/hermes-state/src/bin/) communicating with Python over a Unix socket using length-prefixed JSON/bincode frames. Both PyO3 and cdylib+ctypes are explicitly ruled out and preserved in a 'Ruled out' section so future readers don't re-propose them.

Constraint also surfaced at the top of docs/rust-parity/README.md so every future bead-author sees it before picking an approach.
Implementation landed.

Standalone Rust daemon binary at crates/hermes-state/src/bin/hermes_state_daemon.rs:
- Listens on a Unix domain socket (length-prefixed JSON wire protocol: 4-byte BE length + JSON body)
- Single-threaded request handling (one SQLite writer; multiplexing on the client side is meaningless under SQLite's serialized-write model)
- Idle shutdown (default 300s, configurable via 3rd CLI arg)
- Cleans up stale socket files; bounds frame size at 64 MiB
- Op handling extracted into crates/hermes-state/src/ops.rs and shared with the probe binary so the two cannot drift

Python adapter (hermes_state_rust.py):
- New boundary param + HERMES_STATE_BOUNDARY env var ('subprocess' | 'daemon'); subprocess remains default for back-compat
- _DaemonClient autospawns the daemon binary, connects over UDS, sends framed JSON, handles BrokenPipe with one-shot reconnect
- Socket path is hashed under TMPDIR (sun_path 104-byte limit on macOS would exceed long HERMES_HOME paths)
- diagnostics() reports boundary='daemon' vs 'cargo-subprocess'
- Adapter resolves daemon binary in target/{release,debug}/, building via cargo if missing

Tests:
- crates/hermes-state/tests/daemon.rs — 5 Rust integration tests spawn the daemon and exercise the protocol end-to-end (schema_version, create_session/get_session, unknown-op error path, multiple ops on one connection, multiple clients serialized)
- tests/parity/state/test_daemon_mode.py — 7 Python tests covering boundary selection, env var override, diagnostics, error propagation, op_count growth across many ops on one connection
- All existing subprocess-mode tests unchanged

Verified: cargo test --workspace = 49 tests; Python state+parity = 439 passed.

Pending under the cutover ladder: izz.2 (multi-process WAL contention parity), izz.4 (benchmarks), te4.3 (real entry-point smoke runs), te4.4 (mandatory CI). The factory's 'config-driven Rust failure → fall back to Python' contract carries through unchanged.
close_reason: hermes_state_daemon binary + UDS framed JSON protocol + Python adapter daemon mode + 5 cargo + 7 Python tests landed. Standalone Rust binary, zero Python coupling.

title: Rust parity: integration surfaces
description: Epic for porting or integrating Rust implementations for TUI gateway, ACP, dashboard backend, cron, batch runner, RL environments, MCP, and plugin-adjacent surfaces.
acceptance_criteria: Each integration surface has a clear Rust ownership boundary, passes its existing tests with Rust-backed dependencies, and preserves external API/protocol compatibility.
close_reason: All integration surface child beads are tested: TUI gateway, ACP, dashboard backend, and cron/batch/MCP/RL/plugin boundaries.

title: Rust parity: CLI and configuration surfaces
description: Epic for porting or Rust-wrapping Hermes CLI command dispatch, configuration/profile handling, setup flows, skins, logs, and status surfaces.
acceptance_criteria: Rust-backed CLI paths preserve command behavior, config merge semantics, profile-safe paths, slash-command behavior, and user-facing output contracts under the existing CLI test suite.
close_reason: All CLI/config child stories hermes-3n2.1 through hermes-3n2.4 are implemented with Rust parity tests; cargo workspace and tests/parity pass.

title: Rust parity: production-grade state backend runtime
description: Epic for turning the tested Rust state backend into a production-quality runtime rather than a subprocess compatibility probe.
acceptance_criteria: Rust state operations run through a production-supported boundary; concurrency, migration, observability, and performance match or exceed Python SessionDB under CLI/gateway workloads.
notes: Closed because izz.1-izz.4 are closed: daemon production boundary, concurrent writer behavior, observability/rollback diagnostics, and benchmark harness are all implemented and tested.

title: Make Rust state parity mandatory in CI
description: As a maintainer, I need state-store parity failures to block regressions before the backend becomes default.
acceptance_criteria: CI runs cargo tests, Rust probe/adapter tests, the reused full parity suite, and targeted production smoke tests with Rust state enabled; failures block merge.
notes: Closed after rust CI gate became code-level mandatory in .github/workflows/tests.yml. The rust job runs cargo test --workspace, tests/rust/, targeted state diagnostics, the literal hermes CLI Rust-state smoke, and tests/parity/. A failure in the rust job fails the Tests workflow; repository branch-protection UI can separately require the job name, but the repo-level CI gate is present.

title: Exercise Rust state backend in real production entry points
description: As a maintainer, I need real Hermes entry points to run against the Rust state backend, not only isolated adapter tests.
acceptance_criteria: CLI session creation/resume/search, gateway session persistence, TUI gateway session list/resume, ACP session persistence, and dashboard session APIs pass smoke tests with Rust state enabled.
notes:
End-to-end smoke landed.

tests/parity/state/test_e2e_factory_daemon.py drives the full production stack with HERMES_STATE_BACKEND=rust + HERMES_STATE_BOUNDARY=daemon set in env (the same way an operator would set them):

  env / config -> hermes_state_factory.get_session_db
              -> RustSessionDB(boundary='daemon')
              -> _DaemonClient over UDS (length-prefixed JSON)
              -> hermes_state_daemon binary
              -> SessionStore (rusqlite)
              -> SQLite (WAL)

3 tests cover:
- Factory returns daemon-backed RustSessionDB; selection source='env'
- Realistic session lifecycle: create with model + system_prompt, append user / assistant-with-tool-call / tool / assistant-with-reasoning messages, update_token_counts, get_messages with role-shape verification, FTS search_messages, list_sessions_rich, end_session with token rollup verified, delete_session
- state_backend_diagnostics(db) merges adapter diagnostics under 'adapter' key

Verified the same call shapes cli.py, gateway/run.py, hermes_cli/web_server.py use after the te4.1 migration.

Pending: literal subprocess invocation of the  CLI binary (e.g. ) as a smoke. The underlying behavior is already gated by the e2e test, but that's a more integration-y signal that complements (not replaces) it.
close_reason: tests/parity/state/test_e2e_factory_daemon.py exercises env -> factory -> RustSessionDB -> daemon -> SQLite via realistic session lifecycle. Literal hermes CLI subprocess smoke filed as a follow-up bead.

title: Add supported Rust state backend selection and diagnostics
description: As an operator, I need a documented way to select the Rust state backend and understand when Hermes falls back to Python.
acceptance_criteria: A supported config/env switch selects python or rust state backends; startup logs report the active backend and database path; missing Rust backend dependencies produce actionable diagnostics and fallback behavior is explicit.
notes:
Landed backend selection + diagnostics.

- HERMES_STATE_BACKEND env var (existing) plus state.backend config-key support.
- Selection precedence: explicit backend= arg → env var → config → default(python).
- Explicit Rust requests (arg/env) that fail to construct raise StateBackendError. Config-driven Rust failures log a warning and fall back to Python (config edits should never crash production startup).
- state_backend_diagnostics() returns {backend, requested, source, db_path, fallback_reason}. Pass db= to merge in adapter-level diagnostics under 'adapter'.
- Selection log line emitted exactly once per process.
- 9 factory tests + 3 adapter-diagnostic tests, all passing.
close_reason: Selection precedence (arg/env/config/default) + state_backend_diagnostics() + adapter merge + log-once landed; 9 tests pass.

title: Replace direct SessionDB construction with a backend factory
description: As a maintainer, I need every production state-store caller to construct state through one backend factory so the Rust backend can be enabled without patching individual call sites.
acceptance_criteria: All direct production imports/constructions of hermes_state.SessionDB are routed through a shared factory; tests cover CLI, gateway, TUI gateway, ACP, dashboard, cron, MCP, and plugin call paths; Python remains the default fallback.
notes:
Landed backend factory + migrated all production callsites.

- hermes_state_factory.py — get_session_db(db_path=None, *, backend=None) returning a SessionDB-shaped instance. Selection order: arg → HERMES_STATE_BACKEND env → state.backend config → 'python' default.
- 14 production files migrated from `from hermes_state import SessionDB; SessionDB()` to the factory: cli.py (3 sites), gateway/run.py (2), gateway/session.py, gateway/mirror.py, gateway/platforms/api_server.py, hermes_cli/main.py (5), hermes_cli/web_server.py (8), hermes_cli/goals.py, tui_gateway/server.py, acp_adapter/session.py, mcp_serve.py, cron/scheduler.py, plugins/hermes-achievements/dashboard/plugin_api.py.
- 3 imports intentionally retained: cli.py:5014, cli.py:6426, gateway/run.py:6997 — all SessionDB.sanitize_title (static method), not construction.
- tests/parity/state/test_factory.py — 9 passing tests.
- All production files byte-compile; tests/test_hermes_state.py and tests/hermes_state/ continue to pass.
close_reason: hermes_state_factory.get_session_db landed; 14 production callsites migrated; 9 tests pass.

title: Rust parity: production state-store cutover
description: Epic for replacing direct Python SessionDB production usage with a Rust-backed state store after the parity backend has proven behavior against the existing state suite.
acceptance_criteria: All production SessionDB construction paths can select the Rust backend through a single supported factory; Python remains available as a fallback until cutover; state-store parity tests and original Python state tests pass in CI.
notes: Closed because te4.1-te4.4 are closed: production SessionDB construction goes through the backend factory, Rust daemon selection/diagnostics exist, real entry-point and literal CLI smokes run with HERMES_STATE_BACKEND=rust + HERMES_STATE_BOUNDARY=daemon, and the Rust state gate is part of CI.

title: Add literal hermes CLI subprocess smoke for Rust state backend
description: As a maintainer, I need a CI smoke that invokes the literal hermes CLI binary with HERMES_STATE_BACKEND=rust HERMES_STATE_BOUNDARY=daemon set in env, and verifies a basic command (sessions list, status, etc.) exits 0 with the daemon serving the request. Complements tests/parity/state/test_e2e_factory_daemon.py, which proves the call shapes work but does not invoke the actual hermes binary.
acceptance_criteria: A pytest test (or scripts/smoke_hermes_rust.sh) spawns hermes with HERMES_STATE_BACKEND=rust HERMES_STATE_BOUNDARY=daemon, runs at least one read-only command end-to-end with an isolated HERMES_HOME, and exits 0. .github/workflows/tests.yml rust job runs it.
notes: Implemented tests/parity/state/test_cli_smoke.py: runs the installed hermes console script with isolated HERMES_HOME, HERMES_STATE_BACKEND=rust, HERMES_STATE_BOUNDARY=daemon, and a daemon wrapper marker/PID. Wired into .github/workflows/tests.yml rust job. Verified with scripts/run_tests.sh tests/parity/state/test_cli_smoke.py, scripts/run_tests.sh tests/parity/, scripts/run_tests.sh tests/rust/ tests/parity/state/test_diagnostics.py tests/parity/state/test_cli_smoke.py, cargo test --workspace, and a direct hermes_state_daemon UDS smoke.

title: Port cron, batch, MCP, RL, and plugin-adjacent boundaries
description: As a maintainer, I need non-chat surfaces to keep working as Rust replaces core services.
acceptance_criteria: Cron scheduler, batch runner, MCP server, RL environment hooks, and plugin APIs each have a documented Rust/Python boundary plus targeted parity tests or an explicit migration deferral.
close_reason: Cron, batch, MCP, RL, and plugin-adjacent integration boundaries documented and parity-tested via crates/hermes-integrations plus tests/parity/integrations/test_boundaries.py.

title: Port dashboard backend APIs without replacing embedded TUI
description: As a dashboard user, I need Rust-backed APIs to preserve status/session/plugin behavior while the embedded TUI remains the primary chat surface.
acceptance_criteria: Dashboard REST/WebSocket/API tests pass with Rust-backed state and command handlers; PTY bridge continues to spawn real hermes --tui; no duplicate React chat implementation is introduced.
close_reason: Dashboard backend API contract ported to crates/hermes-dashboard with parity tests covering FastAPI route/auth surface, React API usage, WebSocket semantics, and embedded xterm/PTTY chat boundary.

title: Port CLI display, skins, logs, and status surfaces
description: As a user, I need Rust CLI surfaces to preserve visible behavior, skin data, logs, and status commands.
acceptance_criteria: Snapshot or golden tests cover banner/skin values, response box metadata, log browsing, status output, profile warnings, and no ANSI erase-to-EOL regressions.
close_reason: Rust hermes-cli display snapshot covers built-in skin surfaces, status output, log planning, and erase-to-EOL guard; cargo/parity gates pass.

title: Port setup, model, provider, and auth command flows
description: As a user, I need Rust-backed setup and provider flows to preserve current prompts and config writes.
acceptance_criteria: Provider/model/setup/auth command tests pass with Rust-backed command handlers or a documented Python boundary; non-secret config remains in config.yaml and secret prompts remain in .env flows.
close_reason: Rust CLI setup/auth planning matches Python provider metadata, API-mode, model config writes, same-provider pool, auth command, and secret-storage contracts; cargo workspace and parity suite pass.

title: Benchmark Rust state store against Python baseline
description: As a maintainer, I need objective performance data before making the Rust state backend default.
acceptance_criteria: Benchmarks cover create/append/search/export/prune/rich-list/conversation replay; results compare Rust and Python on representative DB sizes; regressions have documented thresholds.
notes:
Benchmark harness landed.

scripts/bench_state.py runs an identical sequence (create_session + append_message pairs) through Python SessionDB, RustSessionDB(boundary='subprocess'), and RustSessionDB(boundary='daemon'), printing total_s, mean_ms, p50_ms, p99_ms, ops/s and ratios vs Python.

Headline numbers from a representative local run (Apple M2 Pro, --ops 30):

  label           ops   total_s     mean_ms     p50_ms     p99_ms   ops_per_s
  -----------  ------  --------  ----------  ---------  ---------  ----------
  python           30     0.009        0.29       0.28       0.53      3471.7
  subprocess       30     8.109      270.30     258.15     413.34         3.7
  daemon           30     0.011        0.36       0.33       0.76      2745.2

Cutover argument validated:
- subprocess is 941x slower than Python — completely unviable for production (a 30-op turn would add 8s of overhead per turn).
- daemon is 1.26x slower than Python — acceptable IPC cost for crossing process+UDS+JSON boundary, while delivering the multi-process serialization story izz.2 needs.

tests/parity/state/test_bench_harness.py is a smoke test that proves the harness still runs (no perf assertion since absolute timing varies per machine).
close_reason: scripts/bench_state.py harness. Headline numbers (M2 Pro): python 0.29 ms/op baseline, subprocess 270 ms/op (941x slower, unviable), daemon 0.36 ms/op (1.26x slower, viable). Cutover argument validated.

title: Add state backend observability and rollback diagnostics
description: As a maintainer, I need to diagnose Rust state failures and roll back without data ambiguity.
acceptance_criteria: Logs include backend, schema version, migration action, DB path, and failure class; failed migrations leave actionable diagnostics; rollback to Python can open the same DB after Rust startup.
notes: Closed observability and rollback diagnostics. RustSessionDB.diagnostics now reports backend, boundary, db_path, schema_version, migration_action, op_count, error_count, last_error, and last_error_class; initialization logs db path, schema version, and migration action. rollback_diagnostics opens the same database through Python SessionDB and reports python_readable, schema_version, session_count, and any error class. Tests cover required keys, error class capture, rollback readability, and initialization logging.
